title: Non-text Content
chunks: All non-text content that is presented to the user has a text alternative that serves the equivalent purpose, except for specific situations such as controls, time-based media, tests, sensory information, CAPTCHA, or decoration.

Common failures: img elements missing the alt attribute; alt attributes set to empty string on informative images; CSS background images conveying meaning with no text alternative; icon buttons with no accessible name; CAPTCHA images with no text alternative and no audio alternative.

Sufficient techniques: use the alt attribute on img elements to provide a text alternative; mark decorative images with alt equals empty string or role equals presentation; use aria-label or aria-labelledby on icon-only controls; provide an aria-describedby to supplement a short alt text with longer description when needed.

Common ADA lawsuit trigger: e-commerce product images with no alt text mean blind users cannot determine what product they are viewing. Courts have held this creates a fundamental barrier to equal access to goods and services.

title: Audio-only and Video-only (Prerecorded)
chunks: For prerecorded audio-only content, an alternative that presents equivalent information is provided. For prerecorded video-only content, either an audio track or a text alternative is provided that presents equivalent information.
Common failures: podcast clips embedded without a transcript link; silent instructional video with no descriptive text alternative.

Sufficient techniques: provide a text transcript alongside any audio-only content on the same page or via a clearly labelled link; for video-only, provide either a text alternative or an audio description track.

title: Captions (Prerecorded)
chunks: Captions are provided for all prerecorded audio content in synchronized media, except when the media is a media alternative for text and is clearly labeled as such.

Common failures: video files served without a WebVTT or SRT caption track; auto-generated captions not corrected for accuracy; captions hard-coded as image overlays that cannot be styled or turned off.

Sufficient techniques: attach a WebVTT track element with kind equals captions to HTML5 video elements; verify caption accuracy including speaker identification, sound effects, and music notation; correct auto-generated captions that appear in YouTube or Vimeo before publishing.

Common ADA lawsuit trigger: educational platforms, streaming services, and news websites have all faced major litigation for failing to provide accurate closed captions. Settlement amounts have reached $755,000 in class-action cases.

title: Audio Description or Media Alternative (Prerecorded)
chunks: An alternative for time-based media or audio description of the prerecorded video content is provided for synchronized media, except when the media is a media alternative for text and is clearly labeled as such.
Common failures: video tutorials that demonstrate visual-only interactions with no audio descriptions of what is shown; marketing videos where visual branding elements are not described.
Sufficient techniques: add an audio description track to video; provide a text-based media alternative that describes all visual information in the video sequentially.

title: Audio Description (Prerecorded)
chunks: Audio description is provided for all prerecorded video content in synchronized media.
The distinction from 1.2.3: at AA level, a full audio description track is required, not just a text alternative. A text alternative alone does not satisfy 1.2.5.
Sufficient techniques: produce an extended audio description version of videos where pauses are insufficient for in-line description; link to an audio-described version alongside the main player.

title: Info and Relationships
chunks: Information, structure, and relationships conveyed through presentation can be programmatically determined or are available in text. This means that semantic HTML or ARIA roles must convey the same meaning that visual design conveys.

Common failures: using visual headings that are styled div elements with no heading role; form fields not associated with their visible labels via for/id, aria-labelledby, or aria-label; table data presented in CSS-layout div grids with no role equals table semantics; lists rendered as inline spanned items with no ul or ol; required fields indicated only by colour or an asterisk with no programmatic required attribute.

Sufficient techniques: use heading elements h1 through h6 in a logical hierarchy; associate every form input with a label element or aria-label; use semantic table markup with th scope for data tables; use ul and ol for any list of items; mark required fields with the HTML required attribute and supplement with aria-required equals true.

Critical for ADA compliance: this is consistently the most litigated WCAG criterion because screen readers rely entirely on semantic structure. An unlabeled form field is completely opaque to a blind user.

title: Meaningful Sequence
chunks: If the sequence in which content is presented affects its meaning, a correct reading sequence can be programmatically determined.

Common failures: content arranged visually in columns using absolute CSS positioning where the DOM order does not match the visual reading order; footnotes or annotations inserted in the DOM between two related pieces of content.

title: Sensory Characteristics
chunks: Instructions provided for understanding and operating content do not rely solely on sensory characteristics of components such as shape, color, size, visual location, orientation, or sound.

Common failures: instructions saying 'click the round button' or 'see the error on the right'; 'click the green Submit button' where color is the only differentiator; audio alerts with no visual equivalent.
Sufficient techniques: supplement sensory references with text labels; ensure all interactive elements have text names accessible to assistive technologies.

title: Use of Color
chunks: Color is not used as the only visual means of conveying information, indicating an action, prompting a response, or distinguishing a visual element.

Common failures: form validation errors shown only by turning the field border red with no text error message; links distinguished from body text only by color with no underline; required fields indicated only by a red star with no text or aria-required.
Sufficient techniques: pair every color-coded indicator with a shape, pattern, text label, or icon; ensure links have a non-color visual differentiator such as underline.

title: Audio Control
chunks: If any audio on a web page plays automatically for more than 3 seconds, either a mechanism is available to pause or stop the audio, or a mechanism is available to control audio volume independently from the overall system volume.
Common failures: autoplaying video or audio on page load with no pause control visible on screen focus; background music with no volume control.

title: Contrast (Minimum)
chunks: The visual presentation of text and images of text has a contrast ratio of at least 4.5:1, except for large text (3:1 for text at least 18pt or 14pt bold), incidental text in inactive UI components, or logotype text.

Common failures: light-gray body text on white backgrounds in modern minimal design systems; placeholder text using a 60% opacity gray; disabled button text below 3:1 even when informative; text overlaid on photographic hero images without a dark scrim.

Sufficient techniques: use a contrast checker tool against WCAG 2.1 AA ratios during design; ensure design tokens for text colors and background colors all pass 4.5:1 for normal text and 3:1 for large text; do not rely solely on font weight to pass — verify with the actual hex values.

Common ADA lawsuit trigger: low-contrast text is one of the top five barriers cited in ADA web accessibility complaints. Low-vision users represent a large portion of users with disabilities and are disproportionately impacted. Multiple lawsuits have named contrast failures as a primary barrier.

title: Resize Text
chunks: Except for captions and images of text, text can be resized without assistive technology up to 200 percent without loss of content or functionality.
Common failures: fixed-pixel font sizes that do not grow with browser zoom; overflow hidden containers that clip text at 200% zoom; horizontal scrollbars appearing at zoom levels below 400%.
Sufficient techniques: use relative units such as rem or em for font sizes rather than px; test all key pages at 200% browser zoom and verify no content is clipped or lost.

title: Images of Text
chunks: If the technologies in use can achieve the visual presentation, text is used to convey information rather than images of text except for pure decoration or when a particular presentation is essential.
Common failures: banner images containing marketing slogans or calls to action as rasterized text with no HTML text equivalent; navigation links rendered as image sprites.

title: Reflow
chunks: Content can be presented without loss of information or functionality, and without requiring scrolling in two dimensions for vertical scrolling content at a width equivalent to 320 CSS pixels; except for parts of content which require two-dimensional layout for usage or meaning.
Common failures: fixed-width table layouts that force horizontal scroll at mobile widths; side-by-side panels that do not stack at narrow viewports.
Sufficient techniques: use responsive CSS with media queries; avoid fixed pixel widths on containers; test at 320px viewport width.

title: Non-text Contrast
chunks: The visual presentation of user interface components and graphical objects must have a contrast ratio of at least 3:1 against adjacent colors.

Common failures: checkbox and radio button borders that are too light against a white background; icon-only buttons whose icon color does not contrast 3:1 with the button background; input field borders with insufficient contrast against the page background; focus indicators that fall below 3:1.
Sufficient techniques: ensure all UI component boundaries and icons meet 3:1; test with a contrast analyser tool against WCAG 2.1 criterion for non-text elements.

title: Text Spacing
chunks: No loss of content or functionality occurs by setting line height to at least 1.5 times the font size, spacing after paragraphs to at least 2 times the font size, letter spacing to at least 0.12 times the font size, and word spacing to at least 0.16 times the font size.
Common failures: fixed-pixel line heights that cause text overflow when user overrides spacing; components with overflow hidden that clip spaced text.

title: Content on Hover or Focus
chunks: Where receiving and then removing pointer hover or keyboard focus triggers additional content to become visible and then hidden, the following are true: dismissible, hoverable, persistent.

Common failures: tooltips that vanish when the pointer moves off the trigger element before reaching the tooltip; toast notifications that auto-dismiss while the user is reading them; custom dropdowns that close on blur before the user selects an item.

title: Keyboard
chunks: All functionality of the content is operable through a keyboard interface without requiring specific timings for individual keystrokes, except where the underlying function requires input that depends on the path of the user's movement and not just the endpoints.

Common failures: custom JavaScript widgets built only for mouse events with no keyboard event handlers; drag-and-drop interfaces with no keyboard alternative; onclick handlers on div or span elements that are not in the tab order; modal dialogs that trap focus outside the modal.

Sufficient techniques: use native HTML interactive elements such as button, a, input, select where possible; add tabindex equals 0 to custom interactive elements; attach keydown handlers for Enter and Space on custom controls; implement keyboard-navigable alternatives for all drag-and-drop interfaces.

Common ADA lawsuit trigger: keyboard inaccessibility is one of the top three barriers cited in ADA litigation. Users with motor disabilities who cannot use a mouse, as well as blind screen reader users who rely on keyboard navigation, are fundamentally excluded by non-keyboard-operable interfaces.

title: No Keyboard Trap
chunks: If keyboard focus can be moved to a component of the page using a keyboard interface, then focus can be moved away from that component using only a keyboard interface, and, if it requires more than unmodified arrow or tab keys to move focus away, the user is advised of the method.

Common failures: modal dialogs where pressing Tab cycles focus back only within the dialog permanently; third-party chat widgets that capture Tab focus and never release it; embedded PDF viewers that trap keyboard focus.
Sufficient techniques: implement focus trapping within modals intentionally but provide Escape to close and restore focus to the triggering element; ensure third-party embeds do not trap keyboard.

title: Bypass Blocks
chunks: A mechanism is available to bypass blocks of content that are repeated on multiple web pages.

Common failures: no skip navigation link at the top of the page; skip link exists but is permanently visually hidden and therefore not programmatically exposed; skip link target has no tabindex making it unfocusable.

Sufficient techniques: add a visually hidden skip link as the first focusable element on each page that links to the main content anchor; use the main HTML landmark element to identify the main content area.

title: Page Titled
chunks: Web pages have titles that describe topic or purpose.
Common failures: all pages sharing the same generic title tag such as the site name with no page-specific identifier; dynamic SPA pages that do not update the document title on route change.
Sufficient techniques: format titles as Page Name - Site Name; update document.title on every SPA route change using a framework lifecycle hook.

title: Focus Order
chunks: If a web page can be navigated sequentially and the navigation sequences affect meaning or operation, focusable components receive focus in an order that preserves meaning and operation.

Common failures: modal dialog focus not moved to the dialog on open causing screen reader users to be reading behind the modal; SPA navigation leaving focus at the top of the page after loading new content; injected error summary banners not receiving focus programmatically.
Sufficient techniques: move focus programmatically to dialogs, alerts, and new content regions using element.focus(); use aria-live regions for dynamic content that does not warrant a focus change.

title: Link Purpose (In Context)
chunks: The purpose of each link can be determined from the link text alone, or from the link text together with its programmatically determined link context, except where the purpose of the link would be ambiguous to users in general.
Common failures: multiple Read More or Click Here links on the same page with no programmatic differentiation; links whose text is only the URL; icon-only links with no aria-label.

Sufficient techniques: write descriptive link text that conveys the destination or action; where a common label is needed, use aria-label or aria-describedby to add context; add aria-label to icon-only links.

title: Headings and Labels
chunks: Headings and labels describe topic or purpose.

Common failures: section headings that are generic such as Section 1 or Details with no indication of what data follows; form labels that say Enter text with no indication of what to enter; table column headers that are blank or icon-only.

Sufficient techniques: write headings that describe the content of the section they introduce; write form labels that identify the expected input; use scope attributes and meaningful text in table headers.

title: Focus Visible
chunks: Any keyboard operable user interface has a mode of operation where the keyboard focus indicator is visible.

Common failures: the default browser focus outline suppressed with outline equals none in CSS without providing an adequate custom replacement; custom focus styles that are too subtle — for example a 1px dotted border in a similar color.

Sufficient techniques: never set outline equals none on any element without providing a high-contrast custom focus style; WCAG 2.2 SC 2.4.11 Non-text Contrast for focus indicators requires at least 3:1. Apply a solid 2px or thicker outline in a color that contrasts with the adjacent element.

title: Label in Name
chunks: For user interface components with labels that include text or images of text, the name contains the text that is presented visually.

Common failures: button with visible text Search but aria-label equals Find; icon button with aria-label close but adjacent visible label says Dismiss; link whose href aria-label does not contain the visible link text.
Sufficient techniques: when using aria-label, include the visible text verbatim as a substring; prefer aria-labelledby over aria-label to ensure the visible text IS the accessible name.

title: Language of Page
chunks: The default human language of each web page can be programmatically determined.
Common failures: the HTML lang attribute is missing; the lang attribute is set to an incorrect or empty value; SPA frameworks failing to set lang on client-rendered pages.
Sufficient techniques: set lang equals en or the correct BCP 47 language tag on the html element; verify the attribute is present in the rendered DOM for SPAs.

title: Language of Parts
chunks: The human language of each passage or phrase in the content can be programmatically determined except for proper names, technical terms, words of indeterminate language, and words or phrases that have become part of the vernacular of the immediately surrounding text.
Common failures: embedded foreign-language quotes or product names in a different language with no lang attribute on the wrapping element.
Sufficient techniques: wrap any inline foreign-language passage in a span with the appropriate lang attribute.

title: On Focus
chunks: If any component receives focus, it does not initiate a change of context.

Common failures: select menus that submit the form or navigate to a new URL on change event rather than requiring an explicit submit action; dropdown navigation menus that navigate immediately on focus.

title: On Input
chunks: Changing the setting of any user interface component does not automatically cause a change of context unless the user has been advised of the behavior before using the component.
Common failures: checkbox toggles that cause page reload without warning; radio button groups that advance to the next page on selection without a submit button.

title: Error Identification
chunks: If an input error is automatically detected, the item that is in error is identified and the error is described to the user in text.

Common failures: form validation that changes the border color of invalid fields to red but provides no text error message; error markers that are icon-only without text; error messages injected into the DOM in a location not associated with the relevant field.

Sufficient techniques: insert a text error message adjacent to each invalid field or within a summary at the top of the form; associate error messages with fields using aria-describedby; use aria-invalid equals true on fields in error state.

Common ADA lawsuit trigger: inaccessible form error handling is one of the most cited barriers in healthcare, finance, and employment website litigation, as it prevents blind users from successfully completing forms.

title: Labels or Instructions
chunks: Labels or instructions are provided when content requires user input.
Common failures: date fields with no format hint for screen reader users; password fields with no indication of complexity requirements; multi-step forms where step context is not communicated.

Sufficient techniques: provide labels for all form inputs; provide hint text describing required format for complex inputs using aria-describedby; communicate password requirements before the user begins typing.

title: Error Suggestion
chunks: If an input error is automatically detected and suggestions for correction are known, then the suggestion is provided to the user, unless it would jeopardize the security or purpose of the content.
Common failures: a date field in error that says Invalid date with no suggestion of the expected format; an email field that says Error with no hint such as example@domain.com.
Sufficient techniques: include the expected format in the error message; for selection errors offer the closest valid option.

title: Error Prevention (Legal, Financial, Data)
chunks: For web pages that cause legal commitments or financial transactions for the user to occur, that modify or delete user-controllable data in data storage systems, or that submit user test responses, at least one of the following is true: reversible, checked, confirmed.
Common failures: one-click purchase flows with no order review step; account deletion with no confirmation dialog; form submission with no review and confirm step for high-stakes data.
Sufficient techniques: add a review-and-confirm page before completing financial or legal transactions; provide the ability to cancel, amend, or reverse actions for a defined period.

title: Parsing
chunks: In content implemented using markup languages, elements have complete start and end tags, elements are nested according to their specifications, elements do not contain duplicate attributes, and any IDs are unique, except where the specifications allow these features.
Common failures: duplicate id attributes on the same page causing ARIA relationships to fail; unclosed tags in server-rendered HTML; nested interactive elements such as a button inside an anchor.
Sufficient techniques: validate HTML using the W3C Markup Validator; run automated accessibility tests that flag duplicate IDs; avoid nested interactive elements in component templates.

title: Name, Role, Value
chunks: For all user interface components, the name and role can be programmatically determined; states, properties, and values that can be set by the user can be programmatically determined; and notification of changes to these items is available to user agents, including assistive technologies.

Common failures: custom toggle switch built from a div with no role equals switch, no aria-checked, and no accessible name; custom dropdown built from divs with no listbox or option roles; icon button with no aria-label or visible text; accordion using div click handlers with no button role and no aria-expanded.

Sufficient techniques: use native HTML elements when available; when using custom elements add the correct WAI-ARIA role and all required states such as aria-expanded, aria-checked, aria-selected; keep ARIA state attributes updated in JavaScript as user interacts; test with a screen reader after adding ARIA.

Common ADA lawsuit trigger: this is the single most common WCAG failure found by accessibility audits and the most cited in ADA class-action complaints. Any interactive element that is invisible to assistive technologies denies equal access to the interface.

title: Status Messages
chunks: In content implemented using markup languages, status messages can be programmatically determined through role or properties such that they can be presented to the user by assistive technologies without receiving focus.

Common failures: form success messages injected into the DOM without an aria-live region; toast notifications without role equals status or role equals alert; shopping cart count updates with no live region announcement.

Sufficient techniques: add role equals status for non-urgent updates such as item added to cart; add role equals alert or aria-live equals assertive for urgent errors; do not rely on focus movement alone to communicate status.

title: Timing Adjustable
chunks: For each time limit that is set by the content, at least one of the following is true: turn off, adjust, extend. The user can turn off the time limit, adjust it, or be warned before expiration with at least 20 seconds to extend.
Common failures: session timeout dialogs that are not exposed to screen readers; session expiry with no warning at all; time-limited tests with no option to turn off or extend the timer.

Sufficient techniques: display an accessible warning dialog at least 20 seconds before session timeout; allow the user to extend the session with a keyboard-operable button; announce the timeout warning via an aria-live region.

title: Pause, Stop, Hide
chunks: For moving, blinking, scrolling, or auto-updating information, the user can pause, stop, or hide it, or the auto-update can be controlled by the user.
Common failures: hero image carousels and sliders that auto-advance with no pause control; ticker news scrolls with no stop control; auto-refreshing dashboards with no way to pause updates.
Sufficient techniques: add a clearly labeled Play/Pause button to all carousels; provide a stop or hide control for all tickers and scrolling content.
